cover.title: ELK Stack Introduction & __Architecture__
cover.hook: The world's most popular open-source SIEM costs $0. ELK Stack week starts now.

body.text: **ELK Stack** = Elasticsearch (search + storage) + Logstash (ingestion + parsing) + Kibana (visualisation + dashboards). Now called the **Elastic Stack** with the addition of Beats (lightweight log shippers). Used by Netflix, Uber, LinkedIn, and thousands of enterprise SOC teams worldwide.

**Why ELK for security:** It is the most widely deployed open-source SIEM on the planet. ELK appears in 60%+ of SOC analyst job postings. Learning it on a home lab gives you the exact operational experience enterprise roles require — at zero cost.

**Elastic Security** — the built-in SIEM app inside Kibana — includes 2,000+ pre-built MITRE ATT&CK-aligned detection rules, endpoint detection, case management, and timeline investigation. All included in the free basic tier.

slides.label: Data Flow Architecture
slides.method: WHAT — How Data Flows Through ELK
slides.heading: Data Flow: __Log Source to Dashboard__

body.text: **Step 1 — Log Source:** Linux server, Windows host, firewall, web server, or any device generating logs — the raw data origin.
**Step 2 — Filebeat Agent:** Ultra-lightweight log shipper installed on the source. Reads log files and ships them to Logstash or directly to Elasticsearch. Uses ~50MB RAM.
**Step 3 — Logstash:** Receives raw logs, parses unstructured text into named fields using grok patterns, enriches with GeoIP, and forwards structured JSON to Elasticsearch.
**Step 4 — Elasticsearch:** Distributed search engine that indexes and stores all structured log documents. Every field is instantly searchable. Scales from one node to thousands.
**Step 5 — Kibana:** Visualisation and investigation layer. Dashboards, KQL queries, detection rules, alert management, timeline investigations, and case management all live here.

slides.time: 20 min · elastic.co/learn — free official ELK courses

body.text: **Elasticsearch** is a distributed search engine built on Apache Lucene. All data is stored as JSON documents inside indices. Every operation uses a REST API — every query, every index operation, every configuration change is an HTTP call.

# Check cluster health:
curl http://localhost:9200/_cluster/health

# List all indices:
curl http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices?v

# Search all log indices:
curl http://localhost:9200/logs-*/_search

# Count documents in an index:
curl http://localhost:9200/ssh-auth-logs/_count

**Index naming convention:** Use date suffixes — `security-logs-2026.04.23`. Daily rotation keeps search performance high and enables easy data retention policies. Old indices are deleted automatically based on age.

slides.label: Installation Options
slides.heading: Installation: __3 Ways to Run ELK__

body.text: **Docker Compose (recommended for home lab):** Single command starts the entire stack. Clone elastic/stack-docker from GitHub → `docker-compose up` → Kibana at localhost:5601 in 3 minutes. Requires 6–8GB RAM allocated to Docker.
**Elastic Cloud free trial:** 14-day fully managed cloud deployment. No local setup required. Ideal for testing without RAM constraints. Perfect if your machine has less than 8GB RAM.
**Manual VM install:** Install each component separately on a dedicated Ubuntu VM. Most learning value — you understand every configuration file. Requires 8GB+ RAM and 50GB+ disk.

slides.note: **Minimum for home lab:** 8GB total RAM. Set Docker Desktop memory limit to at least 6GB. Less than that causes Elasticsearch to crash silently with no error message.
slides.time: 45–60 min · Install ELK via Docker Compose today

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 64 Checklist
steps: **Install ELK** via Docker Compose — clone elastic/stack-docker → `docker-compose up`
**Open Kibana** at localhost:5601 — log in with elastic/changeme — explore the interface
**Navigate** to the Security app in Kibana — note the Alerts, Rules, and Cases sections
**Check** Elasticsearch is running: `curl http://localhost:9200` — should return cluster info JSON
Understand the **5-step data flow** — log source → Filebeat → Logstash → Elasticsearch → Kibana
slides.time: Total Day 64 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes (installation day)

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 64
steps: **Clone** elastic/stack-docker from GitHub → run `docker-compose up -d` → wait 3 minutes
**Open** localhost:5601 in browser → log in → screenshot your running Kibana home screen
**Navigate** Security → Detections → Rules — count how many pre-built rules are available
**Run** `curl http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices?v` — see the default indices created by ELK

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Being able to say 'I built and operated a home ELK SIEM, ingested real logs, created detection rules, and investigated simulated incidents' is a genuine differentiator at the entry level — not theoretical knowledge.
slides.sub: ELK, Splunk, and Microsoft Sentinel are the three SIEMs that appear in 90% of SOC job descriptions. You are learning ELK this week. Splunk free training is at trailhead.splunk.com. Sentinel has free sandbox labs at learn.microsoft.com. Complete all three and you cover the entire enterprise SIEM market — a combination almost no entry-level candidate has.
slides.mistake: Starting ELK with less than 8GB RAM allocated to Docker. Elasticsearch requires significant memory — it will start without error, appear to run normally, then crash silently when it runs out of heap space. Always set Docker Desktop memory limit to at least 6GB before starting. Check with `docker stats` after startup to verify Elasticsearch is stable.

cover.title: Logstash Pipelines & __Log Ingestion__
cover.hook: Raw logs in. Structured intelligence out. Logstash is the pipeline that makes it happen.

body.text: Every Logstash pipeline has exactly three sections: **input** (where data comes from), **filter** (parse, transform, enrich), and **output** (where enriched data goes). Config files live in `/etc/logstash/conf.d/` and are automatically loaded on startup.

slides.label: Input Plugins
slides.method: WHAT — Where Logstash Gets Its Data
slides.heading: Input Plugins: __Where Data Comes From__

body.text: **Production deployments** almost always use Beats agents → Logstash. Filebeat runs on each monitored system, handles buffering and backpressure, and ships reliably to Logstash's beats input on port 5044.

slides.label: grok Filter
slides.heading: grok: __Parse Any Log__ into Named Fields

body.text:
# Before grok: raw string
# "Apr 23 14:22:01 server sshd[1234]: Failed password for root from 45.33.32.156 port 52341"

# After grok: structured fields
# timestamp: "Apr 23 14:22:01"
# hostname:  "server"
# username:  "root"
# src_ip:    "45.33.32.156"
# src_port:  "52341"

**200+ built-in patterns** cover Apache, SSH, syslog, Cisco, Windows Event Log, and more. **Always test patterns at grokdebugger.com before deploying.** A broken pattern silently tags every event as `_grokparsefailure` and fills Elasticsearch with unstructured, unsearchable data.

slides.time: 30 min · grokdebugger.com — test your SSH pattern now

slides.label: Enrichment Filters
slides.method: HOW — Adding Intelligence to Raw Logs
slides.heading: Enrichment: __Turn IPs into Intelligence__
slides.note: **GeoIP enrichment** is the key to Kibana's geographic map visualisations. Every IP gets latitude, longitude, country, and city automatically — enabling the world map attack visualisation in dashboards.
slides.time: 20 min · Add GeoIP enrichment to your auth.log pipeline

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 65 Checklist
steps: Write a Logstash pipeline that **reads /var/log/auth.log** using the file input plugin
Use **grok** to parse SSH failed login events — extract timestamp, username, and source IP
Add **GeoIP enrichment** to the source IP field — verify geo fields appear in Elasticsearch
**Output** to Elasticsearch with index name `ssh-auth-logs-YYYY.MM.dd`
**Verify** data in Kibana Discover — search the index and confirm all fields are parsed correctly

resources.label: 🔧 Tool
resources.name: grokdebugger.com — Test grok Patterns Before Deploying

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 65
steps: **Test** your SSH grok pattern at grokdebugger.com — paste a sample auth.log line and iterate until all fields parse
**Write** the complete Logstash pipeline config — input, grok filter, GeoIP, mutate, output
**Restart** Logstash: `systemctl restart logstash` — check logs for errors: `journalctl -u logstash -f`
**Verify** in Kibana Discover: create an index pattern for `ssh-auth-logs-*` — confirm fields appear

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Always validate grok patterns at grokdebugger.com before writing a pipeline config. A single broken pattern silently tags ALL events as `_grokparsefailure` — destroying every dashboard that depends on those fields.
slides.sub: Set Logstash JVM heap in `/etc/logstash/jvm.options` to `-Xmx2g` (2GB maximum). Without this cap, Logstash will consume all available RAM as it processes logs — starving Elasticsearch and Kibana of memory and causing the entire stack to become unresponsive. Always cap Logstash heap explicitly in home lab environments.
slides.mistake: Skipping the grok pattern test step and writing pipelines directly in the config file. The iteration cycle for a broken grok pattern is: edit config → restart Logstash (60+ seconds) → wait for events → check if parsed correctly → repeat. At grokdebugger.com, the same iteration takes 5 seconds. Use the debugger for every new pattern, every time.

cover.title: Kibana: Dashboards, KQL & __Visualisations__
cover.hook: Turn millions of raw log events into actionable visual intelligence in minutes. Welcome to Kibana.

body.text: **Kibana Discover** is your primary raw log exploration workspace. It uses **KQL (Kibana Query Language)** for filtering — a powerful, readable query syntax that will become second nature within a week of daily use.

# Essential KQL patterns for security analysts:
event.outcome: "failure"                     # Failed events only
source.ip: "10.0.0.*"                        # IP range
process.name: "cmd.exe" AND user.name: "SYSTEM"  # Multi-field
NOT event.type: "info"                       # Exclusion
http.response.status_code: 404               # HTTP 404s
@timestamp > "now-1h"                        # Last hour
user.name: ("admin" OR "root" OR "Administrator")  # Multiple values
http.request.body.content: "SELECT *"        # SQL injection

**KQL is what you type hundreds of times per day in a SOC role.** Every investigation starts with a KQL query. Master it now and every future platform — Elastic, Splunk SPL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL — becomes easier.

slides.label: Creating Visualisations with Lens
slides.heading: Kibana Lens: __Drag-and-Drop Visualisations__

body.text: **Open Lens:** Kibana → Visualise Library → Create visualisation → Lens (recommended for all new dashboards).
**Bar chart — Failed logins per source IP:** X-axis = `source.ip` (top values) · Y-axis = Count of records · Filter: `event.outcome: failure` → shows top attacking IPs.
**Line chart — Events over time:** X-axis = `@timestamp` (auto-date histogram) · Y-axis = Count → shows traffic spikes and beaconing patterns.
**Metric panel — Alert count:** Single metric, Count of records, filter by `kibana.alert.severity: high OR critical` → real-time high/critical alert count for your monitoring header.

slides.time: 30 min · Build all 4 visualisations from your auth.log data

slides.label: Building a SOC Dashboard
slides.heading: SOC Dashboard: __Your Monitoring Homepage__

body.text: **Kibana → Dashboards → Create dashboard.** Add your saved visualisations by clicking 'Add from library'. Arrange panels by drag-and-drop. Resize each panel to fit.
**Authentication failures by IP** (bar chart) — who is attacking?
**Events over time** (line chart) — when did the spike occur?
**Top 10 source IPs** (data table) — exact IP addresses with counts
**Geographic map of connections** (Maps layer) — where in the world?
**Alert count by severity** (metric panel) — how many critical alerts right now?
**Save the dashboard** as 'SOC Monitoring — Home'. Set it as your Kibana default homepage. Every time Kibana opens, you see the current security status of your monitored systems instantly.

slides.time: 30 min · Build and save your 5-panel SOC dashboard

slides.label: Maps & Saved Searches
slides.method: WHAT — Geographic Maps and Saved Queries
slides.heading: Maps & Saved Searches: __Persistence and Geography__

body.text: **Kibana Maps:** Analytics → Maps → Add layer → Documents → select your index. Plots source IPs on a world map using GeoIP data from your Logstash pipeline. SSH brute-force visualised geographically creates a compelling visual for incident reports and management briefings.

**Saved searches in Discover:** Set a KQL filter → click Save → name it 'Failed SSH logins last 24h'. Saved searches are reusable in dashboards and shareable with teammates. Build a library of saved searches for your most common investigation starting points.

# Build your saved search library:
'Failed SSH logins last 24h'
  → event.outcome: failure AND process.name: sshd

'HTTP 404 mass scanning'
  → http.response.status_code: 404 AND @timestamp > now-1h

'New user accounts created'
  → event.action: user-created

'Privileged command execution'
  → process.name: (sudo OR su) AND event.outcome: success

slides.time: 20 min · Create the 4 saved searches above

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 66 Checklist
steps: Write **5 KQL queries** in Kibana Discover — multi-field, exclusion, IP range, time range, multiple values
Build **4 visualisations** using Lens: bar chart, line chart, metric panel, data table
Assemble a **5-panel SOC dashboard** — save it as your Kibana default homepage
Create a **geographic map layer** from your SSH auth data — see where attacks originate
Save **4 named searches** in Discover — these become your daily investigation starting points

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 66
steps: **Load** Kibana's built-in sample web logs: Home → Try sample data → Sample web logs → Add data
**Build** a 4-panel dashboard: request count over time, top 10 IPs, HTTP response code distribution, geographic map
**Save** it as 'Web Traffic Monitor' — use it to practise dashboard navigation
**Create** 3 saved searches in Discover using KQL — one for failures, one for 404s, one for POST requests

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Master KQL deeply — it is what you type hundreds of times per day in a SOC role. `field: *` checks if a field exists · `field: (val1 OR val2)` matches either · `NOT field: value` excludes · `@timestamp > now-1h` for relative time.
slides.sub: Never hardcode absolute timestamps in saved dashboards. Always use relative time ranges like 'last 24 hours' or 'last 7 days' so dashboards remain permanently relevant without manual updates. An absolute timestamp (`@timestamp: [2026-04-01 TO 2026-04-02]`) is useful only in historical investigations — never in operational monitoring dashboards.
slides.mistake: Building dashboards before verifying data quality in Discover. If your grok parsing failed and fields are not correctly extracted, every visualisation built on those fields will be wrong — showing raw message strings instead of parsed IPs, counts of 1 per message, or empty charts. Always verify field structure in Discover first before building any visualisation.

cover.title: Filebeat & __Log Shipping__ from Real Sources
cover.hook: Filebeat is how logs travel from 1,000 servers to your SIEM in real time. Here is how it works.

body.text: **Filebeat** is an ultra-lightweight log shipping agent that runs on monitored systems. It reads log files, handles backpressure, retries on failure, and ships reliably to Logstash or directly to Elasticsearch. It uses approximately 50MB RAM — negligible impact on production systems.
**The Beats family** — a collection of lightweight shippers for different data sources:
**Filebeat** — reads log files (the most common Beat)
**Winlogbeat** — reads Windows Event Log
**Auditbeat** — reads Linux auditd events with file integrity monitoring
**Packetbeat** — reads network packets (like a lightweight Zeek)
**Elastic Agent** — the modern unified agent replacing all individual Beats

slides.label: Filebeat Modules
slides.method: WHAT — Modules for Common Log Sources
slides.heading: Filebeat Modules: __One Command Setup__

body.text:
# Enable pre-built modules for common log sources:
filebeat modules enable system
filebeat modules enable nginx
filebeat modules enable apache
filebeat modules enable mysql
filebeat modules enable suricata
filebeat modules enable zeek
filebeat modules enable auditd

# Deploy all dashboards to Kibana automatically:
filebeat setup
# Creates index patterns, visualisations, and dashboards
# for every enabled module in one command

# Start Filebeat:
systemctl start filebeat
systemctl enable filebeat

# Verify shipping:
tail -f /var/log/filebeat/filebeat.log

**Why modules matter:** Within 5 minutes of enabling the system module and running `filebeat setup`, you have pre-built Kibana dashboards for SSH login monitoring, sudo usage, and system authentication events — with zero manual configuration.

slides.time: 25 min · Enable system module and run filebeat setup

slides.label: Winlogbeat for Windows Security
slides.heading: Winlogbeat: __Windows Security Events__

body.text: **Winlogbeat** ships Windows Event Log to ELK. Install on any Windows machine to get real-time Windows security monitoring in your Kibana dashboard.

# Critical Windows Security Event IDs:
4624 — Successful logon (who logged in, from where)
4625 — Failed logon (brute force detection)
4688 — New process created (execution monitoring)
4698 — Scheduled task created (persistence detection)
4776 — NTLM authentication attempt
7045 — New service installed (malware persistence)

**These 6 Event IDs cover 80% of Windows attack detection.** A Winlogbeat deployment shipping these events to your ELK stack — combined with Elastic Security detection rules — creates real Windows threat detection in your home lab.
**Configuration:** Edit winlogbeat.yml — set Elasticsearch or Logstash output — run `.\ winlogbeat.exe setup` — start the service. Takes 10 minutes from download to shipping.

slides.time: 20 min · Install Winlogbeat on a Windows VM if available

slides.label: Auditbeat for Linux
slides.heading: Auditbeat: __Deep Linux Monitoring__

body.text: **Auditbeat** ships Linux auditd events to ELK — providing detailed process execution, file integrity monitoring, user session tracking, and per-process network connections. More granular than Filebeat's system module.

# auditbeat.yml — key modules:
auditbeat.modules:
  - module: auditd
    audit_rules: |
      -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity_changes
      -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity_changes
      -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k privilege_changes
      -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -k exec
  
  - module: file_integrity
    paths:
      - /etc
      - /usr/bin
      - /usr/sbin
      - /bin
      - /sbin

**Compliance use:** Auditbeat satisfies PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC2 file integrity monitoring requirements. The file_integrity module detects changes to critical system files — the same monitoring capability that commercial FIM products charge thousands for annually.

slides.time: 20 min · Install and configure Auditbeat on your Linux VM

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 67 Checklist
steps: **Install Filebeat** on your Linux VM — enable the `system` module — run `filebeat setup`
**Start Filebeat** and verify logs are flowing: open Kibana → Dashboards → search 'Filebeat System'
**Understand** the 6 critical Windows Event IDs — when each fires and what attack it detects
**Configure Auditbeat** — add audit rules to monitor /etc/passwd and /etc/sudoers for changes
Verify your **dual-OS monitoring** concept: Linux via Filebeat + Windows via Winlogbeat = enterprise coverage

resources.label: 📺 Videos
resources.name: Elastic Blog — Getting Started with Filebeat

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 67
steps: **Install** Filebeat: `sudo apt install filebeat` — enable system module: `filebeat modules enable system`
**Run** `sudo filebeat setup` — wait for dashboard deployment — check Kibana Dashboards
**Start** Filebeat: `sudo systemctl start filebeat` — verify with `journalctl -u filebeat -f`
**Open** Kibana → Dashboards → 'Filebeat System — SSH login attempts' — verify your own auth.log is showing

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: With Filebeat on your Linux VM and Winlogbeat on a Windows VM, you now monitor both operating systems — exactly what enterprise SOC teams do at scale. This dual-OS monitoring setup is worth explicitly stating in interviews.
slides.sub: In filebeat.yml, always configure either `output.logstash` OR `output.elasticsearch` — never both simultaneously. Having both outputs enabled causes Filebeat to silently drop events without any error in the logs. It appears to be working normally but data never arrives. This is one of the most common and frustrating Filebeat misconfiguration issues.
slides.mistake: Not running `filebeat test config` and `filebeat test output` before starting the service. These two commands validate your configuration file syntax AND verify the connection to your output (Logstash or Elasticsearch) before any events are processed. Running them before `systemctl start filebeat` saves hours of debugging dropped events.

cover.title: Elastic Security: __Detection Rules & Alerts__
cover.hook: Your ELK stack becomes a real SIEM the moment you turn on detection rules. Here is how.

body.text: **Kibana → Security → Rules** contains 2,000+ pre-built detection rules included free in the Elastic Stack basic tier. Every rule is mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques. Enable with a single click — no configuration required to start detecting threats.

**Rule coverage:** Credential access, lateral movement, persistence, privilege escalation, exfiltration, command and control, discovery, initial access — every ATT&CK tactic has dedicated rules out of the box.

**github.com/elastic/detection-rules** — the community rules repository. All 2,000+ rules are open source, viewable, forkable, and contributable. The same rules that protect enterprise environments are available for your home lab and your career learning.

slides.label: Rule Types
slides.heading: Rule Types: __5 Ways to Detect Threats__

body.text: **Query (KQL/EQL):** Evaluates a KQL or EQL condition on a schedule. Fires when matching events exist. Most common rule type — covers 80% of detection use cases.
**Threshold:** Fires when the count of matching events exceeds N within a time window, grouped by a field. Perfect for brute force detection: more than 5 failures from the same IP in 60 seconds.
**Event Correlation (EQL):** Detects sequences of events — the most powerful rule type for catching multi-step attacks that individual event rules miss entirely.
**Machine Learning:** Uses Elastic's anomaly detection on log data — identifies statistically unusual behaviour without predefined signatures. Catches novel attacks.
**Indicator Match:** Matches ingested logs against a threat intelligence feed (IOC list). Automatically alerts when a known-malicious IP, domain, or file hash appears in your logs.

slides.time: 20 min · Explore each rule type in Kibana Security → Rules

slides.label: Creating a Custom Rule
slides.method: HOW — Writing Your First Detection Rule
slides.heading: Custom Rule: __SSH Brute Force Detection__

body.text:
# Custom Threshold Rule in Kibana Security:
# Security → Rules → Create Rule → Threshold

Name:     SSH Brute Force Detected
Index:    filebeat-*
Query:    event.category: authentication
          AND event.outcome: failure

Threshold:
  Group by:  source.ip
  Count:     > 5
  Within:    1 minute

Severity:  High
Risk Score: 73

ATT&CK:   Credential Access / Brute Force (T1110)

Actions:  Create alert + Create case automatically

**Save and enable the rule.** It now evaluates every minute. When more than 5 failed SSH logins from the same source IP occur within 60 seconds, it fires an alert — exactly the brute-force pattern you have been studying since Day 22.

slides.time: 25 min · Create this custom rule in your Kibana instance

slides.label: EQL Sequence Detection
slides.heading: EQL: __Detect Multi-Step Attack Chains__

body.text: **EQL (Event Query Language)** is Elastic's powerful language for detecting sequences of events across time. It catches attackers that individual event rules completely miss — because attackers deliberately spread their actions across multiple events.

# EQL Sequence — Detect recon → enumeration → C2:
sequence by host.name
  [process where process.name == "whoami"]
  [process where process.name in ("net.exe", "netstat.exe")]
  [network where destination.port == 4444
   and not destination.ip: "192.168.0.0/16"]

# EQL Sequence — PowerShell spawning cmd.exe spawning network:
sequence by process.entity_id
  [process where process.name == "powershell.exe"]
  [process where process.name == "cmd.exe"
   and process.parent.name == "powershell.exe"]
  [network where destination.port != 443
   and not destination.ip: "10.0.0.0/8"]
**EQL sequences are the most powerful Elastic Security feature and the least understood by most candidates.** Demonstrate one in an interview and you stand out from 95% of applicants.

slides.time: 25 min · Build and test one EQL sequence rule

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 68 Checklist
steps: **Enable 10 pre-built rules** in Kibana Security → filter by 'Linux' and 'Authentication'
**Create** the SSH brute-force threshold rule exactly as described above
**Simulate** a brute force: `hydra -l root -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://localhost` — watch alerts fire
**Build** one EQL sequence rule — recon → enumeration → outbound connection
Configure an **alert action** — route high-severity alerts to a Slack webhook

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 68
steps: **Enable** 10 pre-built Linux authentication rules in Security → Rules → filter and enable
**Create** the custom SSH brute-force threshold rule with MITRE ATT&CK tag T1110
**Run** `hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt ssh://localhost` on your own VM — watch Security → Alerts
**Investigate** one fired alert — click through to the raw event — document what you see

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: EQL sequence detection is the most powerful Elastic feature and the least understood. Build and demo one EQL sequence rule in an interview and you will stand out from 95% of applicants.
slides.sub: Run Hydra ONLY against your own localhost or an isolated lab VM — never against any other system, even accidentally. Hydra is loud — it fires thousands of authentication attempts rapidly. Running it against any system you do not own or have explicit written permission to test is unauthorised access under cybercrime law, regardless of intent. Always verify your target IP is your own machine before running.
slides.mistake: Enabling all 2,000+ pre-built rules simultaneously on a home lab with limited RAM. Each enabled rule runs on a schedule and consumes CPU and memory. Enabling hundreds at once causes Elasticsearch to become overloaded with rule evaluations, degrading performance across the entire stack. Enable rules selectively — 20–30 at a time — and monitor resource usage with `docker stats`.

cover.title: SIEM Investigation Workflow: __Alert to Report__
cover.hook: An alert fires at 2am. Walk through a complete SOC investigation from alert to written report — inside ELK.

body.text: Every SOC investigation follows the same structured workflow — regardless of company, SIEM platform, or alert type. Knowing this workflow cold, and being able to execute it entirely within Kibana, is the Tier 1 analyst's core job description.
**1. Alert triggers** → Kibana Security detects rule match → alert created with severity, timestamp, affected hosts
**2. Triage** → Tier 1 analyst reviews — is this a true positive or false positive? Is it high priority?
**3. Case created** → formal tracking begins — title, severity, assignee, description
**4. Timeline investigation** → pivot through related events — build the attack narrative
**5. IOC enrichment** → check IPs, hashes, domains against threat intel
**6. Escalate or close** → hand to Tier 2 with full context, OR close with documented disposition

slides.label: Timeline Investigation
slides.heading: Timeline: __Your Forensic Workbench__

body.text: **Open Timeline:** Security → Timelines → New Timeline. Give it a descriptive name: 'SSH Brute Force — 45.33.32.156 — 2026-04-23'.

**Add events:** From Alerts or Discover, click 'Add to Timeline' on relevant events. Pin the critical ones — the first failed login, the successful authentication, the first command executed after login.
**Add analyst notes:** Click any pinned event → Add note. Document why this event matters: 'This is the first successful login after 847 failed attempts from this IP — 4 minutes after attacks began.'
**Save and share:** Save Timeline → attach it to your Case. Every significant investigation produces a named, saved Timeline as the forensic record.

slides.time: 25 min · Build a Timeline from yesterday's simulated brute-force

slides.label: Analyst Pivoting
slides.heading: Pivoting: __Connect Every Evidence Piece__

body.text: **Pivoting** is the core daily skill of a SOC analyst — following evidence connections from one piece of data to the next. In Kibana, every field value is clickable — filter for it or filter it out with one click.
**Alert shows suspicious source IP** → click the IP → 'Filter for this value' → see ALL events from that IP in the last 24 hours.
**Find associated hostname** → click the destination hostname → filter → see all services that host was running when the connection occurred.
**Find processes running at that time** → switch to Auditbeat data → filter by host + timestamp → see every process execution during the attack window.
**Find suspicious parent-child process chains** → filter for the suspicious process → check its parent_process.name → is cmd.exe spawning from Excel? That is malicious macro execution.

slides.time: 20 min · Practise the 4-step pivot chain on your brute-force data

slides.label: Cases Management
slides.method: HOW — Creating and Managing Investigation Cases
slides.heading: Cases: __Full Audit Trail per Incident__

body.text: **Security → Cases → Create case.** Every significant alert generates a case — the formal record of the investigation from first detection to final disposition.

# Case fields to fill:
Title:       SSH Brute Force — 45.33.32.156 — 2026-04-23
Severity:    High
Assignee:    Your Name
Tags:        brute-force, SSH, T1110, credential-access
Description: Automated SSH brute-force attack detected.
             847 failed attempts from 45.33.32.156 over
             4 minutes. One successful login at 14:22:01.

**Add evidence:** Attach the Timeline, add screenshots, link the original alert. **Add notes** at each investigation step — what you found, what you checked, what you concluded. **Close with disposition:** True Positive (attack confirmed), False Positive (benign activity), or Indeterminate (insufficient evidence).
**Cases create the compliance audit trail** — required for PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC2. Every alert reviewed, every investigation documented, every closure with justification.

slides.time: 20 min · Create a complete case for your simulated brute-force

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 69 Checklist
steps: **Open** the brute-force alert from Day 68 — triage it in Kibana Security
**Create** a new Timeline — add 5 pinned events with analyst notes at each
**Pivot** through the investigation: source IP → destination host → running processes → parent processes
**Create** a Case titled 'SSH Brute Force — Lab Simulation' — fill all fields and attach Timeline
**Close** the case as True Positive with a 4-sentence written summary — this is exactly what Tier 1 analysts produce every shift

resources.name: Elastic Security — Investigation and Cases Docs

resources.name: TheHive Project — Free Case Management Alternative

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 69
steps: **Open** the SSH brute-force alert in Kibana Security — triage: is this real or a false positive?
**Create** Timeline 'SSH Brute Force — Lab' — pin 5 events — add a note explaining each
**Create** Case 'SSH Brute Force — Lab Simulation' — attach Timeline — fill all fields
**Write** the 4-sentence incident summary: attack type, source, timeline, disposition — this is your written deliverable

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: The ability to walk an interviewer through 'how I investigate a SIEM alert' step by step — with specific Kibana views, pivoting techniques, case creation, and a written summary — places you above almost every other entry-level candidate.
slides.sub: Practise narrating the investigation workflow out loud until it feels completely natural. Record yourself explaining it. Watch the recording. Refine until you can walk through alert → triage → timeline → pivot → case → report in under 3 minutes with specific, confident details. That narration is what separates a technically competent candidate from one who actually gets the job offer.
slides.mistake: Closing alerts as False Positive without documenting WHY they are benign. Undocumented FP closures are how real attacks get missed during incident review. Every FP closure must explain: 'This alert fired because of X. I investigated by checking Y and Z. I determined it was benign because of W.' That documentation protects you professionally and builds institutional knowledge for your team.

cover.title: ELK Week __Recap__ & SIEM Certifications
cover.hook: ELK week done. You built a real SIEM from scratch. 70 days in. Phase 8 preview: Cloud Security.

slides.label: ELK Week Full Recap
slides.heading: 7 Days of ELK: __What You Built__

body.text: **Day 64:** ELK architecture — Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Beats data flow. Docker Compose deployment. Elastic Security overview.
**Day 65:** Logstash pipelines — input plugins, grok parsing, GeoIP enrichment, mutate transforms, Elasticsearch output with daily index rotation.
**Day 66:** Kibana Discover — KQL queries, Lens visualisations, 5-panel SOC dashboard, geographic maps, saved searches library.
**Day 67:** Filebeat modules (system, nginx, auditd), Winlogbeat for Windows Event IDs, Auditbeat for file integrity, Elastic Agent as the future.
**Day 68:** Elastic Security detection engine — 2,000+ pre-built rules, 5 rule types, custom threshold rule, EQL sequence detection, alert actions and routing.
**Day 69:** Full SOC investigation workflow — Timeline forensic workbench, 4-step analyst pivoting, Case management with disposition, written incident summary.

slides.time: 30 min · elastic.co/learn — complete free courses this week

body.text: **Elastic Certified Analyst (ECA)** — the most relevant certification for security analysts. Tests Kibana investigation, detection rule creation, alert triage, and incident investigation. Directly validates exactly the skills built this week.
**Elastic Certified Engineer (ECE)** — cluster management, pipeline engineering, performance tuning. More DevOps-oriented. For security engineers who deploy and maintain ELK infrastructure.
**Both are proctored, practical exams** — not multiple choice. You perform real tasks in a live Kibana environment. No memorisation tricks — actual operational skill is tested.

**Strategy:** Complete Elastic's free training at elastic.co/learn first — especially 'Getting Started with Elastic Stack' and 'Elastic Security Fundamentals'. Then practise in your home lab until ECA tasks feel effortless. The ECA exam directly mirrors what you built in Days 64–69.

slides.label: Splunk — The Market Leader
slides.heading: Splunk: The __Market Leader__ Worth Learning

body.text: **Splunk** is the most widely deployed enterprise SIEM globally. Average salary for Splunk-skilled SOC analysts: **$90–110k**. It is the single most mentioned SIEM in job postings — by a significant margin over ELK.

**Splunk uses SPL (Search Processing Language)** — conceptually similar to KQL but with different syntax. ELK knowledge transfers directly — the concepts of ingestion, parsing, searching, alerting, and dashboards are identical. The syntax is the main learning curve.

# KQL vs SPL comparison:
# KQL (Kibana):
event.outcome: failure AND source.ip: *

# Splunk SPL equivalent:
index=main event_outcome=failure | stats count by src_ip

# Both achieve the same investigation — different syntax
**Free training:** trailhead.splunk.com — complete the 'Intro to Splunk' and 'Splunk Core User' paths. Both are free. The Splunk Core User certification validates foundational SPL skills.

slides.time: Start Splunk free training at trailhead.splunk.com today

slides.label: Microsoft Sentinel
slides.method: WHAT — The Third Major SIEM Platform
slides.heading: Microsoft Sentinel: __Cloud-Native SIEM__

body.text: **Microsoft Sentinel** is a cloud-native SIEM/SOAR platform built on Azure. It uses **KQL (Kusto Query Language)** — nearly identical to Kibana's KQL — making the transition straightforward after this week.

**Job market reality:** Azure + Sentinel skills command $95–125k in the current market. The SC-200 (Microsoft Security Operations Analyst) certification covers Sentinel deeply and is highly valued in enterprise environments that run Microsoft infrastructure.

**The three SIEMs to know:** ELK (built this week) + Splunk (learn next) + Sentinel (after Splunk). Together they cover 85%+ of enterprise SIEM deployments globally. Knowing all three places you in the top 5% of security operations candidates — a combination almost no entry-level candidate has.
**Free sandbox:** learn.microsoft.com provides free Sentinel lab environments. The SC-200 study guide includes hands-on labs at no cost.

slides.time: Review SC-200 exam objectives at microsoft.com/learning

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 70 Checklist
steps: **Complete** Elastic's free 'Getting Started with Elastic Stack' course at elastic.co/learn — save completion
**Sign up** for Splunk free training at trailhead.splunk.com — complete 'Intro to Splunk' module

**Write** the resume bullet point: 'Built and operated a home SIEM using Elastic Stack — ingested Linux logs via Filebeat, created 10 custom KQL/EQL detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, and conducted complete incident investigations using Kibana Timeline and Case management'
**Document** your ELK home lab setup on GitHub — README with architecture diagram, components, and detection rules built
**Research** SC-200 and ECA certification requirements — plan which to pursue first

resources.label: 🎓 ELK
resources.name: elastic.co/learn — Free Official Elastic Certifications Prep

resources.label: 🎓 Splunk
resources.name: trailhead.splunk.com — Free Splunk Core User Training

resources.label: 🧪 Path
resources.name: TryHackMe — SOC Level 1 Learning Path (all 3 SIEMs)

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your __Final ELK Week__ Task
steps: **Complete** Elastic free course at elastic.co/learn — 'Getting Started with Elastic Stack' (2–3 hours)
**Start** Splunk at trailhead.splunk.com — complete 'Intro to Splunk' (1 hour) — dual SIEM experience begins
**Document** your ELK home lab on GitHub — architecture, components, detection rules — link from LinkedIn
**Write and save** your ELK resume bullet point — 'Built and operated a home SIEM...' — ready to paste into applications

stats.label: Days of consistent learning — 78% of the journey done
Fully operational home SIEM built from scratch at zero cost
Custom detection rules created and tested against simulated attacks
Days remaining — Cloud Security starts tomorrow

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Write this resume bullet now: 'Built and operated a home SIEM using Elastic Stack — ingested Linux authentication logs via Filebeat, created 10 custom KQL/EQL detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, and conducted complete incident investigations using Kibana Timeline and Case management.' That single bullet wins interviews.
slides.sub: ELK is not the only SIEM you will encounter. The concepts — ingest, parse, search, alert, investigate — transfer identically to Splunk, Sentinel, QRadar, and every other platform. The person who understands why each step exists will adapt to any new SIEM platform in days. The person who only memorised Kibana clicks will struggle every time the tool changes. Learn concepts deeply.
slides.mistake: Treating the three SIEM platforms as separate things to learn from scratch. ELK, Splunk, and Sentinel all do the same thing — collect logs, parse them, search them, alert on them, and help you investigate. The workflows are identical. The syntax differs. Master the workflow once on ELK, then the other two are just vocabulary lessons.
